main_ingredients: teriyaki sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese rice topped with teriyaki-glazed salmon, thinly sliced beef, and a half boiled egg, served with miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked (FDC ID 175168); Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174032); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424); Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (FDC ID 168878); Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238); combined in estimated proportions for a 500 g bowl.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central values for the component foods in proportions consistent with a typical 500 g teriyaki salmon and beef donburi bowl: approximately 200 g cooked rice, 100 g cooked salmon, 80 g cooked beef, 50 g half boiled egg, 40 g teriyaki sauce, 30 g other. All component values drawn from USDA Foundation Foods or SR Legacy entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (short-grain, cooked) GI ~54–72; composite dish GI estimated at 54 based on rice-dominant carbohydrate profile moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 54 (low) using short-grain white rice as the primary glycemic driver (GI 54–72 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021). The lower end of the rice GI range was selected because co-ingestion of protein (salmon, beef, egg ~10.8 g/100 g) and fat (4.6 g/100 g) attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (54 × 81 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 43.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from teriyaki sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Each 500 g serving provides approximately 1550 mg sodium, representing over 67% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce options or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (54) but high glycemic load (43.7) per serving from short-grain white rice, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the estimated glycemic index is low (54), the large serving size results in a high glycemic load of approximately 44; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should practice strict portion control, especially for the rice component, and monitor postprandial glucose, noting that the protein from salmon, beef, and egg helps attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon balanced against sodium load from teriyaki sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon provides cardioprotective long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which support cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from teriyaki and soy sauce (~1550 mg per serving) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who require strict sodium restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Teriyaki Salmon and Beef Bowl (半熟卵照り焼きサーモン牛丼, Hanjuku Tamago Teriyaki Saamon Gyudon) is a Japanese donburi dish consisting of a bowl of steamed short-grain Japanese rice topped with teriyaki-glazed salmon fillet, thinly sliced simmered beef, and a soft half boiled egg. The teriyaki glaze is made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, imparting a sweet-savoury umami flavour to the salmon. The beef is typically cooked in a lightly seasoned broth in the gyudon style. The half boiled egg (hanjuku tamago) has a fully set white and a soft, jammy, partially liquid yolk that enriches the bowl when broken. The dish is served with miso soup or a drink as a set meal. Texturally, the bowl combines the soft stickiness of Japanese rice, the flaky tenderness of glazed salmon, the silky beef, and the creamy egg yolk. Nutritionally, the dish is a protein-rich meal (~54 g protein per 500 g serving) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon, moderate carbohydrate from rice, and a moderate glycemic load estimated at approximately 44. Sodium content is elevated due to teriyaki sauce and soy sauce (~1550 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate glycemic load is partially attenuated by the co-ingestion of protein and fat. The dish is available at Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore, including Sukiya.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central component-food entries in proportions consistent with a typical 500 g teriyaki salmon and beef donburi. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using short-grain white rice as the primary glycemic driver, moderated by protein and fat co-ingestion. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in USDA FoodData Central and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Values should be updated if an HPB Singapore or equivalent regional database entry becomes available. 2. Serving size of 500 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese donburi set meal portions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component-food data and are not directly reported by a single source for this dish. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; teriyaki sauce and beef sourcing at the listed restaurants have not been independently verified. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (gyudon) is used as the closest parent; the dish is a hybrid donburi not fully captured by any single FoodEx2 term. 7. No -1 sentinel values require special justification beyond the standard note that these micronutrients are estimated from component-food data rather than directly reported for this dish; all numeric values are positive estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central and are not set to -1.
